text.title: Hybrid Search Implementation Rules
text.summary: Rules for Qdrant native hybrid search, dynamic alpha selection, BM25 preprocessing requirements, and incremental index update constraints.

rule.statement: Use the vector database's native hybrid search endpoint when available (Qdrant, Weaviate, Elasticsearch) rather than calling two endpoints and fusing client-side.
rule.rationale: Native hybrid search executes both retrievers in a single server-side call, reducing latency. Client-side fusion requires two round-trips and local merge logic.

rule.statement: For Qdrant sparse vectors, pre-compute BM25 or SPLADE sparse encodings before insert. You cannot pass raw text to a sparse vector endpoint.
rule.rationale: Qdrant's sparse vector format requires pre-encoded sparse representations. Attempting to pass tokenized text directly produces a type error at the API level.

rule.statement: Apply domain-specific stop words for BM25. Generic English stop words hurt technical corpora where words like "the" may appear in product names.
rule.rationale: Standard stop word lists remove common words that may carry meaning in technical domains. For example, "The Architect" as a product name would lose its key token.

rule.statement: Rebuild the BM25 index when documents are added incrementally. Some vector databases do not support live BM25 updates.
rule.rationale: BM25 IDF scores are corpus-wide statistics. Adding documents without rebuilding the BM25 component produces stale IDF values that degrade keyword scoring for both old and new documents.

rule.statement: Compute alpha before the retrieval call, not after. An agent that decides alpha post-retrieval cannot change the already-returned result set.
rule.rationale: Alpha determines the retrieval mix at query time. It must be computed from the query characteristics before the retrieval call is made.

example.code: def compute_alpha(query: str) -> float: """1.0 = pure semantic, 0.0 = pure keyword.""" if '"' in query: return 0.2 # Quoted exact phrase if any(c.isdigit() for c in query): return 0.35 # Contains codes/numbers if len(query.split()) <= 3: return 0.5 # Short specific query return 0.7 # Long conceptual query
example.note: Alpha is computed from query characteristics before the retrieval call. Heuristics are starting points; tune against a labeled query set.

antipattern.description: Using equal weights (alpha=0.5) for all query types without per-domain tuning.
antipattern.reason: A mistuned alpha underperforms pure vector search on semantic-heavy queries and pure BM25 on keyword-heavy queries. Equal weighting is the starting point, not the optimal value for most domains.

text.reference: Alpha selection logic based on query characteristics
